author: @sharonrose9552
text:
@ThinkingandTinkering   Is this also the 'dark energy' that tesla predicted? 

-The resistance is lower on the wire the faster you pulse it which is why HVDC works.
 -Pulsed DC current can be used in a transformer. 
-Pulsed DC can be pieced together to an AC waveform by energizing 'steps' and increasing the number like inverters that produce square waves, modified sine waves, and full sine. 
-A transformer coil typically uses two sets of windings to step up or down voltage. BUT we also more windings can be added and the efficiency of the energy caught by the 'secondaries' is increased but it isn't cost effective and unnecessary in most applications. 
-The field collapsing is just the individual coil, working as a capacitor, and releasing energy. 
-An AI to the algorithm, could be used to make sure all the coils are working together to produce the correct AC sinewave based on the timing of the fields collapsing, as well as, frequency/voltage regulation (sagging) issues created by the load. 
-there is greater efficiency because you are only supplying exactly enough energy to fill the coil so the collapsing releases all the energy. There is more energy captured with the extra windings then with a standard transformer. 

Which leads to the quesiton 1700% efficiency compared to what which I never saw mentioned in their literature?  (you mentioned 17,000 at the beginning of your video btw.),  
The larger questions to me is Did they actually find new laws, or are they just different formulas based on the laws?  What are the ridges (thin and thick regions) in the coils? How much heat does it produce? how much external EMF is escaping the system? 

  
All in all, even if it achieved overunity, the cost of making the item, is going to have a -very- long payback period and there are components that can easily fail like thyristors or mosfets, and even in HVDC the cost of thyristors is the cost prohibitive piece because they use expensive metals, are expensive to produce and only last around 15 years. Sometimes it is cost prohibitive to be more efficient which is hard to explain to the Global Warming crowd.

replies.text:
En la segunda mitad del siglo XVIII se formularon las primeras teorías sobre el origen y la historia de la Tierra. Una de las más importantes fue la de James Hutton, un geólogo y naturalista escocés, que en 1785 y 1788 propuso la siguiente teoría: 
 
La Tierra es un mundo-máquina, creada para ser habitada por la humanidad. 
 
El calor interno de la Tierra es el responsable de las transformaciones de la materia, no el agua. 
 
La Tierra tiene un ciclo geodinámico externo, que Hutton trazó. 
 
La Tierra tiene una geodinámica interna, que Hutton propuso. 
 
Hutton también desarrolló el concepto de Tiempo Profundo, que se refiere a la intemporalidad de la Tierra, donde pequeños cambios graduales pueden lograr casi cualquier cosa. 
 
En esa época, la teoría más debatida era la que consideraba que el Diluvio fue el factor geológico más importante en el modelado de la superficie terrestre.

My meaning is to look at the power put into the system, and the heat that it generates at the output.

Afterwards it seems that he used a backup power pack, that charged and used low power, but put more power out. It’s clear that this didn’t ran for a long time. But testing was done over a period of minutes, not hours. I used a 10 kHz data acquisition system, and the output voltage was chopped, so difficult to measure. author: @walterbaltzley4546
text:
About the only term this "development" throws out there that makes any real sense when it comes to energy amplification is COP (Coefficient of Performance) - Which indicates how much power you get out versus how much energy you put in.  We have had the ability to achieve a COP of 10 or greater for decades using readily available technology that violates ZERO scientific "laws":  It is called a refrigerator, or more precisely a refrigeration heat-pump.   

How does it work?  It extracts heat from inside your refrigerator and moves it to the outside.  A simple refrigeration pump can move 10 watts of heat (or more) for every watt of electricity you use to run the pump.  Note that the word is MOVE heat, not produce or generate heat.  The energy is already present in the atmosphere, all that we are doing is removing it from the air inside the refrigerator, and concentrating it in the condenser - which then gets released into the air outside the refrigerator.  

However, if we wanted to, instead of simply releasing that heat into the open air we could potentially use that energy to heat water - transferring the energy from inside the  refrigerator, and storing it in a tank.  We could then either use the hot water directly, or use a heat-engine to convert 30-40% of that heat into electricity.  This recycling of heat is commonly referred to as "regenerative" cycling.  

Now do the math on that - put 1,000 watts of energy into a heat-pump with COP 10, this allows us to move 10,000 watts of heat.  Lets assume 10% loss in transfer.  This leaves us with 9,000 watts of heat.  Then we use a heat-engine to convert 30% of the remaining energy to electricity:  That gives us 2,700 watts of electricity or 2.7x the amount of electricity out than we put into the system.  The rest of the energy is released to the ambient atmosphere and ultimately makes its way back to the refrigeration side of the system.  

This system generates enough energy to run itself and produce a surplus, provided there continues to be extract-able heat in the ambient atmosphere.  This is the fundamental principle behind what Tesla called a "Self-Acting Engine" - A virtually limitless source of energy that ultimately draws its power from the SUN, which provides heat to the atmosphere.  Given the right working fluid (refrigerant) we can extract heat from the atmosphere at temperatures below -40F, making it suitable for use even in Antarctica.

The electrical energy comes from the magnetic field. It becomes a bit confusing, there are different descriptions, zero point, ether, back emf etc. 
The idea is simple and it is the difference between generating power in one winding or in two as in a transformer. There is a gap in the magnetic circuit, the magnetism builds and then the current in the other winding drives down, diminishes the field into the other polarity to gain a large output.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

author: @allanvrc725
text:
Magnetic link in a transformer is 1/2500th of the DC equivalent in an AC 80watt output. About 40 amp turns is in the link. The current in one winding with a load is 80/240=0.333 R.M.S. and this is canceled out by the other winding because the currents flow like a differential. If there are 1350 turns then 1350 *0.333=449.55amp turns which is impossible to achieve. 
Electric power should be generated in a transformer.

author: @allanvrc725
text:
Having worked on this type of generator there is a simple explanation. A generator output operates with the strength of the magnetic field and the rate of change of the magnetic field. When one winding is deployed any current created from a changing magnetic field will create the effect of a counter emf to oppose the current being generated. This is similar to pushing two of the same magnets' poles together. The maximum amount of force is required to drive a rotating field in a generator.

I use 50 to 80 watts of power at my house continuously. When an 80 watt AC transformer is tested the magnetic field in the link between two windings can be determined to be a very small DC equivalent in fact it is 1/2500th of the 80watt load. (80/0.032watt=2500). The transformer works like a differential where the currents go in the opposite direction in each winding, of course the magnetism cancels out. But one winding has a leading current and is the input and the current is ever so slightly more than the other for 1 quarter cycle and then this changes to where the output current is greater. 

When the currents are maximum and opposite only the very low wire resistance is apparent but as the magnetism builds up in each half cycle the current slows to a stop and this produces the voltage. It is similar to a water ram pump. At one small instant of time very small currents add together to maximize the magnetism and then there is the change of direction of the current for the next half cycle.  

The 240 volt winding is about 1350 turns therefore the 80 watt transformer operates with 40 ampere turns. This can be injected into the transformer core by having uneven turns and using another inductor to force more ampere turns in one of the windings. The windings are connected together correctly on one end and then current can flow into one winding and then back through the other while the turns are a different amount and this should not exceed 10% difference. A floating power input isolates the power injection from the high voltage of the input and output windings. There is also a gap in the magnetic circuit of the main transformer but no gap in the other inductor. 

As few as 300 turns are necessary because the voltages can be too great other wise. 

I believe Nicola Tesla built this idea into his magnifying transmitter for some one to figure out. The large coil is vibrated and resonates by atmospheric electricity, current flows and this produces magnetism and then the primary is pulsed so that the current is large enough to instantly reverse the magnetic polarity. The large currents then slow to a stop and this produces voltage. The magnifying transmitter enhances the result into huge voltage swings.

author: @beforebefore
text:
The one way to get an honest output power measurement from any such system, is to rectify it to DC, filter it for minimal ripple, and THEN use DC Current and Voltage measurement.

But many will claim their system won't work if rectified... but that's just the cop-out... because today's semiconductors make this easy and inexpensive.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: nice one mate

replies.author: @beforebefore
replies.text:
The rectifier losses are very straightforward to account for, and add back into the math for final output power.  Filter capacitors only get hot if they are wrong for the application... this is where my 40 years of EE circuit design help. And yes, I'm an alternative energy researcher, experimenter, builder, and believer... I've spent over 15 years at it... just don't call it "free energy"... it's always going to be [energy conversion]... that "free energy" term is what really bothers the PhD's.  The origin of the energy may well be a source that's not completely understood or described/defined by modern academia, but that doesn't mean it's not there... such as Dark Matter and Dark Energy.

REAL Science doesn't scoff at the unknown... investigating "the unknown" is where we begin learn new things. Many of today's "over-educated" tend to think/assume that Modern Science knows "all there is"... and that anything without a pre-existing definition can't be real... that's arrogance, and to be honest... stupidity.

BTW, a load resistor... being completely passive, is still the best way to measure output power... but the equipment required to isolate and accurately measure the total energy dissipated in that resistor is somewhat cumbersome... and expensive.

author: @pa4tim
text:
All those meters are most correct while measuring amps and volts that are sinewaves around 50 Hz. Bandwidth is often a few kHz with a pretty low crest factor. Measuring something that produces strong EM fields and a signal that is far from ideal can make those measurements useless. I have seen cheap meters showing 3 times the real voltage when used on a SMPS. The only good way to measure this set-up is using a very good scope first as a reference. This way you can see what is going on and if you can trust the measurements using the chosen multimeters and clampmeters. 
Above that, DC Clamp meters are for most a bad choice in unknown situations, the hall sensor is very bad in situations with large magnetic fields. Seeing the set-up, I very much doubt the results.

author: @mfr58
text: Yes, there have been many such claims and you've sussed this one out. However, we do need to accept that nothing in science is provable, it's only falsifiable. So laws of physics are only laws as long as we don't find something that disobeys it, which may happen at some point. A scientific law is derived from the way we question nature, nature will reflect back a pattern from which we develop a working model, but it only relates to the framework of investigation we've employed. If we asked nature a different question or in a different way, we may well see another pattern and create a different law.
